content.text: 他开业十分得法。他进这个医院很费了心思，目的是为了更远的前途，想得到美国大夫贾克逊的赏识，可以赴美镀金。然而他不是这个医院的正统毕业生，所以也受所谓正统派的、更为贾克逊赏识的医生们的排挤。他是一个矮个子，脸上白里透——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他开业十分得法。他进这个医院很费了心思，目的是为了更远的前途，想得到美国大夫贾克逊的赏识，可以赴美镀金。然而他不是这个医院的正统毕业生，所以也受所谓正统派的、更为贾克逊赏识的医生们的排挤。他是一个矮个子，脸上白里透红，十分光——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 红，十分光——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 尤大夫？英语意思是……请进。英语意思是——

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我刚从外边回来，里边有什么消息没有？我们这个医学院还办不办？

theme: 一方介绍尤大夫的职业发展困境与外貌特征，另一方刚归来便询问医学院是否继续开办的消息

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你来啦？那么进来谈吧。江教务长，徐主任，你们好。江道宗？好。董院长。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 董院长，我们很欢迎你来。你对医院的改革，我们是拥护的。可是这是个有历史的医院，医院立下许多规矩，都是有道理的。譬如说吧，病人家属，过去院长从来不见的。他们最麻烦，愈管他们，意见愈多。要照从前，像贾大夫病人这种事情——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 徐主任，你对贾大夫病人这种事情怎么看？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 全院都知道，贾大夫人顶好了，从来没治死过病人。我得说句公道话，这是病人家属无理取闹。工人阶级，我也说好，可是赵铁生这个工人，他没有知识，他一点不懂科学。

theme: 董院长到访医院，徐主任阐述医院旧有规矩的合理性，并就贾大夫相关的病人家属纠纷发表看法，认为是家属无理取闹

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 昌荃，你还叫他想什么？我看你不是帮助你舅舅，你这是成心毁他！我知道你自小就不喜欢我们。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 是的，我是不喜欢你们，你们也不喜欢我。我总觉得我们是两种人。我记得小时候在你们家里，每次病了，总是把我放到顶楼上。有一次一连三个月没有人理我，那时我才十三岁。我倒是有过这样的想法，我想我大了决不理你们。可是我长大之后，受了另外一种教育，这种教育告诉我，世界上——

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 比个人的感情更重要的东西。今天我来的确是为了帮助舅舅，要你明白过来，做一个真正的中国人。这个计划我留在这里，我还会来，只要你觉得我能帮助你。

theme: 一方指责昌荃意图毁掉舅舅，昌荃回应双方自幼的隔阂过往，表明此次前来是为帮助舅舅，希望对方能做真正的中国人

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 木兰，让我们高兴的事就这么几件吗？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 哦，还有吗？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你爸爸就要走了，你还不好意思对他说？我不赞成你以后写信告诉他。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 不，我害怕。我看还是你说好。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 好，说就说。昌荃，你知道吗？宋阿姨从安东来信了，里面还有给爸爸的。她说她明天就要过鸭绿江，欢迎爸爸赶紧来。那封信爸爸看了会更高兴了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 昌荃，董院长来电话。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 好，我一会儿就来。（袁仁辉、何昌荃下。凌木兰擦凌士湘的显微镜。外面有汽车的声音。凌士湘由外上。他一夜没有睡，在反细菌战展览会紧张地翻阅着各种材料，这时疲乏地回到家中。）

theme: 两人商议向即将离开的父亲分享高兴的事，提及宋阿姨的来信，后因董院长来电及凌士湘疲乏归家中断对话

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 嗯，也留点力气增加生产，永远红光满面。小圆头鼻子，一脸青胡根，大嘴一咧，就露出一口洁白整齐的牙齿。头顶已经半秃，稀疏的头发向后拢过去。他穿着工厂制服，现在正立在右窗前面，叉着腰，弯身向外瞭望。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 这个要人有个当头！一会儿说坐着轮船来了，一会又说坐汽车来了。来了来了，他还是没有来！俺们这些猴儿孙，足足等了一点一刻钟，这就是七十五分，四千五百秒！全公司四厂三室六大处，还有俺这小小的炼铁部，厂长、处长、主任，停止工作都到齐了等！

theme: 一方描述工厂内某人物的外貌与状态，另一方抱怨等待要人耗时过长，公司各部门人员均停工等候

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 不，卢秘书，轮船上带来了。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 哦，人家自己带来的？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 是副厂长。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 去去去！谁叫你们把这些桶子、瓶子也都搬进来？这是洋厨房！叫你们把桌布铺上，玻璃杯摆好！一群死猪，抬出去！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 快快快！摆在这儿，笨！桌布这么铺，放好！眼睛！拿玻璃杯，摆上，擦干净，摆齐！（凭着一个转弯亲戚的面子做了官，投入何湘如的麾下，以善于吹拍捧骗、嘴上伶俐乖巧赢得上司的欢心，很快地钻到现在的地位。一脸——）

theme: 关于物品来源的简短交流后，一方指挥下属布置洋厨房，斥责下属办事笨拙，同时提及自身的升职背景

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 爸爸，凌伯伯。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 承灿，方才我忘记告诉你，你的老师最近从美国给我写一封信，又问到你。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 哦？说什么，凌伯伯？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 他非常关心你的事业。（念信）“战时银行贷款工业，如遇相当数目，须呈财政部核准，始可贷款。”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 是你告诉他，你已经在中国设计成功高周波电炉么？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 没有，爸爸，现在这只能算是个试验。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 反正他知道。他很惊我们在后方居然装起这么一个最进步的炉子。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 可是高速度钢出了以后，并没有市场。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 那不管，他很高兴。他说他很骄傲，有你这么一个学生。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 哼，他不知道，我们又从贝斯麦炉做起了。

theme: 凌伯伯向承灿提及美国老师的来信，交流高周波电炉试验、高速度钢市场及贝斯麦炉相关的钢铁事业话题

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 听，一定是二先生来了吧？自然哪，这点儿股……我说这个股子，这个——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 总经理，何董事长已经到了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 一共是三辆汽车。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 还有其他的客人。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 现在都请到会客厅里了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 听说是何先生来了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 是协理。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 好，我们去看看。请，请。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 不起，尽管——

theme: 众人议论何董事长一行到访的相关情况，包括车辆数量、客人安排及人员身份，Speaker2话语未完

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 光斗留小子的时候，跟我一同在欧洲乱跑，就一直地好放炮。不过人真是光明磊落，越老越有精力。就像现在这一次坚决辞职，简直对他毫无办法。我一连看他几次，他强到底不肯回来，弄到后来，这个套子拴在我的头上，真是再也想不到的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 反正规模大致已经有了，尤其是跟夫合作——

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 谈到难关，何董事长，我觉得我们可以这样分析：第一，我认为资本不能增值，所利税太重；第二，我感觉到运输太困难，一切统制不够合理；第三，我认为非常遗憾的，国家建设事业太少，钢铁成品只好放在库里生锈；第四，最急的，也是置公司于死——

theme: 一方谈及光斗的过往经历、辞职事件及自身的处境，另一方向何董事长分析公司发展面临的四大难关，话语未完

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 对了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 喝，真热！工友！田先生！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你把地上那堆围裙、手套、包脚布、草，立刻送给吹钢股颜先生，请他在这张单子上签字盖章，跟着就拿回来。工友：是。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 这会儿还好，起重机没出什么乱子。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 按说，出点乱子也不能怪。这么热的天，又是新手，更容易昏头。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 小王，这一会寒暑表多少度？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 一百零二。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我的天！还是晚上十一点哪！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 哼，就跟白天一样热。

theme: 车间夜间高温，一方安排工友送物品并询问温度，两人感慨炎热天气，同时提及起重机及新手操作的相关情况

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 沈，你对他们太好，太好！在我钢厂，这样子不成的。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 这是少数从乡里来的庄人，他们慢慢就学会工厂人的习惯，慢慢就会养成一种新的意识、新的看法。这些基本的规矩，自然就渗到他们的脑筋里。告诉他们，教他们，接近他们，他们都可以成很好的工人。现在中国没有那么多的技工，农人们能来肯来，难道因为一个个没有经验，就都赶出去么？提到中国工业化问题——

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 就说过，他说——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 得了，沈，你的老师到德国来，我见过他。哈，你的老师是个理想家，大炮，激烈分子，一个不是正路的钢铁专家。用他们美国话说，他才真正是一个——

theme: 双方就对待农民出身工人的管理态度产生分歧，沈主张培养教导，另一方则不认同，还评价沈的老师是理想家

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 哎呀！炉子又向下倒！别跟着跑！人工回转的人，别跑！刘海青，回去摇！别怕！别——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 钢水！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 坑里有水！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 水！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 快躲开！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他上去了！梁麦米，他在帮着摇！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 承灿！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 炉子已经管不住了，你们离开！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 快来！火星！钢在空中划出千万道光彩，穿过钢梁，由顶上洒入望台与室内。砂子！砂子！垫砂子！

theme: 炼钢炉发生倾倒危机，现场众人紧急呼喊应对，发现坑内有水后提醒躲避，有人主动帮忙尝试控制炉子

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 石清，你回来了？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 早来了。我听说您正跟报馆的人谈天，所以没敢叫人请您去。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 李太太有事么？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 没有事，没有事。你还是进去打牌去吧。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 报馆有什么特别关于时局的消息么？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你不用管，叫你买的公债都买好了么？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 买了，一共二百万，本月份。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 成交是怎么个行市？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 七七五。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 买了之后，情形怎么样？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我怕不大好。外面有谣言，市面很紧，行市只往下落，有公债的都抛出，可是您反而——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我反而买进。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 您自然是看涨。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我买进，难道我会看落？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 经理，平常做存货没什么大危险，再没办法，我们收现，买回来就得了。可现在情形特别，行市一个劲儿往下跌，要是平定一点，行市还有翻回来的那一天，那您就大赚了。不过这可是由不得我们的事。

theme: 经理与石清商议公债买卖事宜，石清担忧市面行情持续下跌，经理却坚持看涨并大量买进公债

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以你想，你要跟他做个好事，叫他平平气。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我这次可许了他了，只要他当了电影明星，我就想法子嫁给他。我跟你痛痛快快说吧，我都想过：画报上一定登那么老大的照片，我的，胡四的，我们两个的；报纸每天登着我们蜜月的新闻。并且——陈白露，恭禧恭禧恭禧！你现在又觉得结婚有意思了？我得好好吃你一杯喜酒！不过，你的大学小姐呢？休了？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 嗯，胡四当了电影明星就大不同了。我叫胡四在她的什么慈善游艺会，以电影明星的资格唱个浪漫歌，跳个胡拉舞，你看她不乐得飞飞的？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 八姐，我一定替你办。你真聪明，想得真周到。我答应你，我一定找潘四爷，明天就设法叫他入电影公司。好吧？

theme: 八姐向陈白露讲述与胡四的约定，称胡四成为电影明星后便嫁给他，陈白露答应帮忙找潘四爷促成胡四进入电影公司

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 请说清你的姓名、军阶和目前职务。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 朱尼厄斯·汉纳福德·厄本，美国海军凯恩号战舰，三等信号兵。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你认识桌子那边坐着的那位军官吗？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 哪一个？那有俩儿呢。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 你认识桌子那边坐着的那位军官吗？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 说出你认识的那一个。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 执行官呗，长官。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 哪一个？那有俩儿呢。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 他的姓名。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 说出你认识的那一个。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 玛瑞克先生。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 执行官呗，长官。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 他的姓名。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 在哪里执行？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 船上。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 玛瑞克先生。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 船名是什么？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 在哪里执行？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 凯恩号。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 船上。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 谢谢。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 船名是什么？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 对不住您了，长官。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 凯恩号。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 谢谢。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 对不住您了，长官。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 朱尼厄斯·汉纳福德·厄本，美国海军凯恩号战舰，三等信号兵。

theme: 对美国海军三等信号兵朱尼厄斯·汉纳福德·厄本进行询问，核实其个人身份、对船上执行官玛瑞克的认识及船名等信息

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 凶手一定是在办公室关门前、保全系统脆弱的时候进去的。古尔德加班到很晚。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 他明白，他是城里最大的辩护律师。其实他很无——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 尸体是周二早上发现的。事务所周一关着门，所以凶手可能周五就进去了，一直等到清洁工离开。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 律师古尔德被绑起来，右手放开，手里拿着屠夫的刀子。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 看看天……

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: “一磅新鲜人肉。”

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: “一磅人肉，不多不少，没有软骨，没有硬骨，只要鲜肉。”——出自《威尼斯商人》。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 没读过。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: “只要割了肉，他就可以自由。”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我告诉你，那把刀满了汗水。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 那是必然的。凶手想让古尔德慢慢来决定，到底先切哪一块肉。想象一下，一把枪对着你的脸——

theme: 两人讨论辩护律师古尔德被杀案件，分析凶手的作案时间与手法，提及相关台词出自《威尼斯商人》，并推测凶手的作案心理

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 那个恶心的男人吗？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 侵入图书馆系统，并且记录信息很多年了。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 记录罚款吗？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 不，监视大众阅读习惯。听着，有些书会被标记，比如说和核武器有关的书、《我的奋斗》，如果——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 慢着，这不是违法的吗？

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 合法？违法？他们可管不了这么多。我们没法直接查到这些信息，只是起到指导性作用而已。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 所以说，他们有这个名单。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 说的对。如果你想知道谁在读《炼狱》、《失乐园》、《杀人王曼森》，电脑会告诉我们的。能——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 或许是一个大学的孩子在写期末论文，主题是二十世纪的罪行。

theme: 两人谈论图书馆系统被非法侵入，相关方长期监视大众阅读习惯，标记特殊书籍并形成阅读名单，一方对此提出合法性质疑

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 好，开始！刀！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 好，手术完了！

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 两小时二十六分钟——啊，门癌手术的新纪录！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 托，托您的福，他捡了一条命。我一直没敢跟我们当家的说，在九州的时候，医生说这是非常难的手术，所以才到这儿来的。可是托先生、托先生的福……

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 啊，再拖几天就危险了。的确是很难的手术，可你们当家的运气还不错。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 您这么一说，我们实在是……等过些日子，我们再郑重地向您道谢。实在是起死回生啊！我，我简直不知道该怎样向您道谢才好。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 手术后的保养很重要啊，希望你们特别注意照顾好病人。

theme: 医生完成创纪录的门癌手术，患者家属向医生表达深切感激，医生叮嘱家属重视术后保养事项

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 照你这不世故、头脑单纯的意见，可就——

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 不世故？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 当然嘛！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 既然是当不成教授，不当它不就完了么？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 什么？！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 财前君，我只问你一句话：那个佐佐木庸平的病情怎么样？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我没有受你指挥的必要！正在采取万无一失的办法。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 癌症加上肋膜炎，依目前为止的医学水平，会有什么有效的治疗方法呢？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 怎么没有？！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 你有卓越的才能，为什么对于医学以外的事那么热中？而且——

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你是个光知道啃试管、显微镜、书本的虫子！可是在活人的世界上啊，医学或学问可也是活的！

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 财前君！财前君！佐佐木庸平是你的患者呀。

theme: 财前与对方就教授职位竞选及患者佐佐木庸平的病情治疗方案产生争执，对方指责财前热衷医学以外的事务

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 首先有请双方进行开篇立论，计时三分钟的陈词，有请正方。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 哈利波特为什么会踏上那段伟大的冒险呢？其真实原因是她在晚会里过得并不开心。如果他过得特别好，我相信他可能就未必选择那个去霍格沃茨的入学通知书了。所以我们会看到很多伟大的作品当中，那些主人公踏上伟大的征程的时候，一定是因为他们或多或少处于他们的哈利波特时刻。我想问，那你我现在处于这样的哈利波特时刻吗？其实我就在哈利波特时刻里。我今年三十五岁了，我在经济不太景气的房地产业工作，我会担心自己还不起房贷，会担心自己的工作不保。当然在很多人看来，我能还起一套房的房贷已经是足够的幸运，连我这种足够幸运的人都觉得自己如此惶恐不安。我相信在座的年轻人就更加心有所感，你们要面对我毕业时更难的竞争，更大的压力，更高的高考分数线以及更长的工作时间。我觉得你们也在面临自己的哈利波特时刻。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你应该找一个自己爱的人结婚。为什么呢？茫茫人海中能够找到一个我深爱的人结婚、白头偕老，谈何容易？所以我一旦拥有，就会更加珍惜，更加珍爱呵护这段来之不易的感情。这样会使我们学会理解，学会包容，学会负责，让我们认真完美，让两个人的生活多姿多彩，谢谢大家。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 对于这个话题，必须先明确婚姻和恋爱的区别。恋爱是一种双方相互吸引的短暂的激情，而婚姻是一种建立在家庭之上，接受并包容一切对对方，以及对方一切的长期责任。恋爱是理智决定的结果，不仅仅是爱和激情。对方辩友强调的主要是感情，但是我是一个经济专业的学生，从一个概率的角度来看，我总会选择我爱的人。请大家看这个摄意图，由于身体不对称，我们只能知道自己的感觉，结婚之前不可能知道对象是否真心爱我们。结果不管你的对象怎么选择，选择你爱的人，总比选择爱你的人更符合经济学利益最大化的原理。

theme: 选你爱的人还是爱你的人

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 各位，首先先由正方发起一分半的陈词。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 各位好，什么叫做童年的消逝？童年的消逝在我方的第一个视角下，叫做某一个高高在上的视角的消失。前两天，链家贝壳找房的创始人左晖去世了，他在因为癌症去世前接受过一个采访，说鼓励他努力整顿中介行业的一个动力，是有一天他的女儿问他：“我的同学说，你是北京最大的房屋中介头子，什么是房屋中介？”他发现他难以回答和面对自己女儿的这个问题。所以，什么叫做童年？童年是悬挂在每一个成年人头顶上的达摩克利斯之剑。当我们做出每一个行为的时候，我们会想，如果我的孩子在身边，他会怎么看待？我们在成年世界里充满无奈。

theme: 童年的消逝时表示悲剧

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 因为前人类丧失了内在良知，却还浑然不觉？还是因为钱？人心价值和人性尊严都被当作了牺牲品，供奉到了拜金主义的祭坛之上。钱作为工具的确可以促进社会经济的发展，但问题就在于，现实中它已经被人们当作了目的本身来对待。但是当崇尚自由的人类精神已经被附上了黄金锁链的时候，它还能自由飞翔吗？谢谢。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 大家晚上好。正方辩友刚才告诉我们，钱之所以是万恶之源，是因为他把钱等同于目的了。那我想请问二辩一个问题：我今天奉公守法地去追求钱，钱也可是我的目的了，请问钱就成为万恶之源吗？第二地方辩友告诉我，钱有时不是万恶之源，是不是有法律的制约？我想请问各位：法律是基于钱不赚，还是制约我们行为的准则呢？若是这些行为的准则，那钱还是万恶之源吗？接下来请让我们开宗明义，解释一下钱的几个重要定义。报钱其实是人类文明发展的产物，它是一个不具主动性的交易媒介。而“二”则是一个价值上的破坏、行动上的破坏，而我们所谓的“源”，就是事情的起源和根本。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方今天想说，刚好相反——爱应该是自由意志的苏醒。我不要你，因为你离不开我，所以对我好；我要你基于自己独立的想法对我好。所以我们会说“结为金兰”，而不会说“沦为金兰”。我们会祝福你步入婚姻，而不会说你“落入婚姻”。所以在这个世界上，我们歌颂的不是那些沦为傀儡的时刻，而是机器人获得人心的时刻。谢谢。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 大家远道而来听听爱情。好的感情就是长期稳定的幸福，而且它不是天上掉下来的，它是彼此共同用心经营出来的。我方认为彼此独立，就是谁都不依赖谁，两个人有谁没谁都一样。而彼此依赖呢，并不是24个小时一直要黏在一起，而是两个人的人生轨迹相互交融。所以独立和依赖讲的都是心理状态。

theme: 好的感情让人更独立还是更依赖

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为，金钱不能买到一切。金钱只能作为交换的媒介，它无法买到真正的情感、健康，甚至无法买到时间本身。比如，金钱可以买到医生的治疗，但无法保证病人的完全康复；可以买到朋友的陪伴，但无法买到真正的信任。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方认为，金钱在现实中几乎可以解决大部分问题。只要你有足够的钱，你可以改善生活条件、接受教育、获得医疗保障，甚至通过物质手段影响社会关系。金钱虽然买不到某些抽象的东西，但在实际生活中，它的作用已经非常接近万能。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 但是你要明白，金钱能买到的都是有限和有条件的东西，它无法替代人的价值观和内心的选择。你花再多的钱，也买不到一个人真正的尊重和理解，更买不到失去的时光。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 可是正因为有限性，才体现了金钱的价值。通过合理运用金钱，我们可以最大化资源利用，实现生活目标和社会效率。从经济学角度看，金钱的力量并不亚于其他任何资源。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方坚持，真正的人生意义和幸福，是金钱无法直接买到的。金钱只是手段，而非目的本身。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方认为，既然金钱能实现绝大多数需求，它在实践中近似万能，因此我们不能低估它的重要性。

source: ai 帮我生成一些正反双方进行辩论的样例，大概在1分钟左右，格式就是和之前一样Speaker1:xxx Speaker2:xxx 这样

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为，人工智能虽然在某些领域展现出了惊人的效率，但它永远无法完全取代人类的工作。工作不仅仅是完成任务的过程，更是一种创造力、判断力以及情感交流的体现。即便机器能够处理大量数据，完成标准化操作，它也无法像人类一样理解复杂的社会背景、体会人类的情感需求，或者在突发情况中进行灵活应变。比如，一个客服机器人可以回答固定问题，但它无法体察客户的焦虑与情绪，也无法给予真正的心理安慰，而这些正是人类工作不可替代的部分。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方则认为，人工智能在未来很可能取代大多数重复性和低复杂度的工作，从而显著提升社会效率。无论是制造业的流水线作业，还是金融领域的数据分析，AI都能够以比人类更高的精度、更快的速度完成任务，减少人为错误和成本支出。甚至在创意领域，AI已经能够生成艺术作品、撰写文章，虽然可能缺少情感深度，但在数量和效率上，人类远远无法匹敌。因此，在大多数行业里，人工智能的替代性是不可忽视的。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为，社交媒体在现代社会造成的负面影响远大于其正面作用。虽然它能够带来信息传播的便利和社交的便捷，但过度依赖社交媒体已经改变了人们的认知模式和生活方式。大量的虚假信息、舆论压力以及碎片化的注意力，让人们更容易陷入焦虑和孤独。比如，青少年过度沉迷于社交平台，很可能出现社交比较焦虑、自尊心下降等心理问题，这种负面影响并不是社交媒体所能弥补的。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方认为，社交媒体在现代社会的作用利大于弊，它为信息共享和社会沟通提供了前所未有的便利。通过社交媒体，人们可以跨越地域界限获取资讯、分享观点，甚至参与公益和社会治理。举例来说，在自然灾害或公共事件发生时，社交平台能够让信息迅速扩散，救助资源及时调动，这些都是传统媒介无法比拟的优势。只要合理引导使用，社交媒体的正面效应远超过其潜在风险。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为，鼓励年轻人创业虽然表面上看充满激情与机会，但实际上风险巨大，过度创业可能对年轻人的人生规划带来不可逆的伤害。创业不仅需要资金，还需要丰富的经验、人脉和心理承受能力，而这些都是大多数年轻人尚未具备的。举例来说，许多年轻人为了创业放弃稳定工作，却最终因为市场波动、管理不善或者团队冲突而破产，导致经济压力和心理压力双重叠加。因此，盲目鼓励年轻人创业是不负责任的行为。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方则认为，年轻人正处于人生的黄金时期，鼓励创业能够激发他们的创新力和抗风险能力。创业本身不仅是追求经济利益，更是对个人能力和社会资源的整合训练，它能够让年轻人学会独立思考、解决复杂问题，并在失败中积累宝贵经验。虽然风险存在，但正是这种风险带来的学习机会，使年轻人能够成长为更有担当、更具创造力的社会个体，因此鼓励创业具有积极意义。

theme: 应该鼓励年轻人创业吗

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为在线教育无法完全替代传统教育。你方一直强调在线课程便捷、高效，但这忽略了教育的核心——互动、情感交流和批判性思维的培养。教师面对学生时的即时反馈、个性化引导和课堂气氛，是任何线上平台难以复刻的。即便技术再先进，也无法弥补师生面对面的心理和情感连接。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方不同意。你方高估了传统教育的不可替代性，忽视了在线教育的灵活性、可扩展性和资源均衡优势。对于偏远地区、特殊人群，在线教育让他们享受到优质教育资源，这些都是传统教育无法实现的公平性和效率。你方的论点只看到了课堂情感，却否认了现实社会的教育不平等问题。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你方所谓“公平性”，只是看到了资源分配，却没有考虑教育的深度和质量。线上课程缺乏互动讨论和实践操作，无法培养学生综合能力和团队协作意识，这种教育缺陷对长远社会发展是致命的。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 你方过分强调互动情感，却忽视了技术进步带来的教育创新，如虚拟实验、AI辅导和在线测评。这些技术能够弥补甚至超越传统课堂的某些局限，你方对技术潜力的否定过于保守，缺乏现实基础。

theme: 在线教育能替代传统教育吗
source: 辩论中应该包含对对方论点的攻击

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为环保必须优先于经济发展。你方一直强调经济增长的重要性，但如果忽视环境问题，短期的经济利益将导致长期的生态崩溃，最终反而阻碍经济的可持续发展。你方的逻辑存在严重短视，只关注GDP数字，却没有看到空气污染、水资源枯竭和气候变化对社会整体的毁灭性影响。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方不同意你方观点。你方把环保绝对化，忽视了经济发展对民生和社会稳定的基础作用。如果经济停滞，社会贫困和就业压力将加剧，环保目标也将无法实现。你方提出的“长期生态利益”在现实政治经济中根本无法完全落实，是理想化的空想。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你方所谓现实无法落实，是对技术和政策潜力的低估。全球已有多国通过绿色经济和可再生能源实现了经济增长和环境保护双赢，这说明环保优先不仅可行，而且对经济长期稳健更有益。你方片面强调短期利益，忽视了长期可持续发展的根本逻辑。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 你方的例子只是少数试点，无法代表大规模应用。发展中国家在经济起步阶段仍需依赖传统能源和高消耗产业，你方的理论无法解决这些国家现实发展需求，忽略了环境与发展之间的现实冲突，这才是必须考虑的问题。

theme: 教育优先，还是经济发展优先？

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为大学教育不应只追求所谓的实用技能，你方把教育简化为“就业工具”，完全忽视了大学塑造人格、培养批判性思维和创新能力的核心价值。你方片面追求就业率，等同于把大学变成培训班，这种短视逻辑不仅扭曲教育本质，更会严重削弱社会未来创新的可能性。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方不同意你方的高尚论调。你方把大学理想化成“思想圣殿”，却忽视了现实社会的需求。如果大学毕业生无法胜任岗位技能，只能沦为纸上谈兵，这种教育最终是失败的。你方高谈理想，却对经济现实视而不见，这完全是空想主义的表现。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你方所谓现实需求，只是眼前资本市场的短期利益，并不能代表社会长远发展。社会需要能够独立思考、解决复杂问题的人才，而非只会执行任务的技能工人。你方这种功利化教育观，是扼杀未来创新的根源。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 你方的“长远发展”听起来冠冕堂皇，却缺乏操作性。现实是残酷的，毕业生如果无法融入社会，所谓“批判性思维”只能成为空洞口号。你方的理想化观点完全脱离了社会运作的逻辑，这才是真正的教育失败。

theme: 大学教育应该培养实用技能吗

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 我方认为人工智能对人类就业并非绝对威胁，你方一直在渲染“AI取代一切岗位”的恐慌，却忽视了技术发展带来的岗位升级和新职业的诞生。正如工业革命没有摧毁劳动力市场，而是推动了劳动结构优化，AI同样会让劳动者转向更高价值、创造性更强的岗位。你方这种夸大恐惧的论调，只会让人们对技术产生无谓恐慌，完全没有事实依据。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 我方不同意你方的轻描淡写。你方所谓“岗位升级”，在现实中意味着大量低技能劳动者将被直接淘汰，而他们不具备再就业的能力。你方把少数精英的受益经验当作普遍规律，完全忽略了社会的不平等和技能鸿沟，这种论点不仅天真，而且极具误导性。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: 你方把技术发展妖魔化，只会阻碍社会适应变革的能力。如果我们因为害怕AI而停滞创新，真正受损的不是机器，而是整个社会的生产力和竞争力。你方的观点实际上是在鼓励保守和退缩，而不是面对现实和寻找解决方案。

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: 你方所谓“面对现实”，实际上是为资本利益辩护，而牺牲了普通劳动者的生存权利。技术再先进，也不能用“长远好处”来合理化眼前的失业和社会不公，你方这种观点既冷漠又不负责任。

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: March rate cuts seem to be off the table or less likely, and the market’s reacting to that. Big tech is front and center—Meta, Apple, and Amazon are reporting after the bell, so will their results be more mixed than Microsoft and Google’s? Those two had solid earnings but failed to impress. Before diving into tech, we need to talk about financials. We’re seeing banks we’ve never heard of making headlines—one in Japan, another in New York. Commercial real estate has loomed over the market for ages, so are we finally seeing a reality check there? Is this another regional bank crisis in the making, or is that just fiction? Most say no, since New York Community Bank’s issues are idiosyncratic due to its holdings. But the real concern is the unresolved reckoning in commercial real estate, worsened by Japan’s Aozora Bank dropping twenty percent on U.S. property losses. It’s the commercial property “shoe that hasn’t dropped,” not a banking collapse. Also, many banks’ commercial real estate assets aren’t marked to market—they planned to hold them to maturity hoping for improvement, but maturity is near (end of two thousand and twenty-four, start of two thousand and twenty-five). Will we see more banks raising provisions soon?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: You’re spot-on about the key risks—this isn’t a broad regional bank crisis. New York Community Bank’s troubles stem from bad commercial real estate loans, which forced it to set aside five hundred and fifty-two million dollars in provisions (ten times analysts’ expectations) and slash dividends by seventy percent . It’s a unique case, not a systemic issue. But commercial real estate is absolutely the powder keg—Aozora Bank’s plunge comes from extra provisions for U.S. office loans and bond sale losses . Banks have been hiding losses by holding assets to maturity instead of marking them to market , but with maturities hitting in late two thousand and twenty-four-early two thousand and twenty-five, they can’t delay anymore. Office vacancy rates are high (Manhattan’s at fifteen percent ), property prices are crashing, and lenders face mounting bad loans. We’ll definitely see more banks boosting provisions to cover these losses—this is just the start of commercial real estate’s impact on the financial sector

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Worldwide. This is Bloomberg Surveillance alongside Lisa Brambitz together with Anne-Marie Hauden. I'm Jonathan Farrow. Your equity market on the S&P five hundred bouncing back from the biggest one-day loss since September. Equity futures on the S&P five hundred positive here by a third of one percent. Brammo, pushback from Chairman Powell. Big earnings coming a little bit later.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Yeah, basically taking March off the table, maybe, or making it less likely. The market's responding to that. Big tech very much front and focus. And the real question to me is, do we get a more motley picture with the likes of Meta, with the likes of Apple and Amazon reporting after the bell than we did with Microsoft and Google, which put out solid earnings, but people just weren't impressed. Before we talk about tech, we need to talk about the financials. We seem to be at the face of the cycle where we talk about banks we've never heard of before. One in Japan, another in New York. Commercial real estate is something that's been hanging over this market for a long, long time. Are we starting to see a little bit of a reality check on that side of things? So the reality versus the fiction, the fiction that some people might say is, is this another crisis in the making in regional banks? And most people say no. New York Community Bank Corp was an idiosyncratic story with respect to the mix of holdings. That said, you have serious

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Worldwide. This is Bloomberg Surveillance alongside Lisa Brambitz together with Anne-Marie Hauden. I'm Jonathan Farrow. Your equity market on the S&P five hundred bouncing back from the biggest one-day loss since September. Equity futures on the S&P five hundred positive here by a third of one percent. Brammo, pushback from Chairman Powell. Big earnings coming a little bit later.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Yeah, basically taking March off the table, maybe, or making it less likely. The market's responding to that. Big tech very much front and focus. And the real question to me is, do we get a more motley picture with the likes of Meta, with the likes of Apple and Amazon reporting after the bell than we did with Microsoft and Google, which put out solid earnings, but people just weren't impressed. Before we talk about tech, we need to talk about the financials. We seem to be at the face of the cycle where we talk about banks we've never heard of before. One in Japan, another in New York. Commercial real estate is something that's been hanging over this market for a long, long time. Are we starting to see a little bit of a reality check on that side of things? So the reality versus the fiction, the fiction that some people might say is, is this another crisis in the making in regional banks? And most people say no. New York Community Bank Corp was an idiosyncratic story with respect to the mix of holdings. That said, you have serious concerns about a reckoning that has not happened in commercial real estate. And those were just exacerbated with the Azora Bank in Japan reporting a twenty percent drop, just seeing a twenty percent drop in their shares as a result of U.S. property losses. So again, it's the shoe that hasn't dropped in commercial property, not necessarily fear.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Feed-in for Asian markets with a tech seller of driving US stocks to a fourth session of losses. NVIDIA slipping ahead of its third quarter results as Wall Street worries about a potential AI bubble. But the big deals in the sector keep coming. NVIDIA and Microsoft to invest up to fifteen billion dollars in Anthropic, which in turn will spend thirty billion dollars on Microsoft computing capacity. And President Trump's grip on Washington shows signs of weakening as the Senate reaches a deal to release the Epstein files after near unanimous House support. Welcome to the Bloomberg New Economy Forum two thousand and twenty-five, thriving in an age of extremes. Of course, we have seen US tariffs at the highest levels since the nineteen forties at a time when we also have the ongoing US-China tech rivalry. What's your take on these intertwined dynamics?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: And of course, right now, the focus and the hottest topic, artificial intelligence. And the gap is being fast closed by the Chinese side. But there are these lingering questions. Is there only one form of AI leadership? And what does the AI future look like? As you talked about tariffs, it's also this backdrop of, is it too soon, about, you know, ten months into the presidency, to tell how the Trump policies are actually reshaping the global trading order? Is it really beneficial to the US in terms of this pecking order

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Stories this hour. Asian stocks set to extend Wall Street losses as investors shun risk ahead of Nvidia's earnings and the US jobs report. The S&P five hundred falling below its fifty-day moving average while Bitcoin traders position for more pain. Amazon joins a spree of jumbo debt sales from tech firms racing to fund AI infrastructure, raising fifteen billion dollars. Plus, bond king Jeff Gunlock tells Bloomberg he's loading up on cash and staying away from private credit over what he calls garbage lending.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Alex, welcome. I am so happy to be talking to you today.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Thanks for having me. I've known you for quite some time. I am so excited to be on the podcast.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Why don't we start at the beginning, just for a broader audience? Talk a little bit about the founding story of Scale.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Right before Scale, I was studying AI and machine learning at MIT. And this was the year when DeepMind came out with AlphaGo, and Google released TensorFlow. So sort of maybe the beginning of the deep learning hype wave or hype cycle. And I remember I was in college. I was trying to use neural networks. I was trying to train image recognition neural networks. And the thing I realized very quickly is that these models were very much so just a product of their data. And I sort of played this forward and thought through it. And these models, or AI in general, is the product of three fundamental pillars: there's the algorithms, the compute, and the data. And at that time, it was clear there were companies working on the algorithms, labs like OpenAI, or Google's labs, or a number of AI research efforts. NVIDIA was already a very clear leader in building compute for these AI systems. But there was nobody focused on data. And it was really clear that over the long arc of this technology, data was only going to become more and more important. And so in two thousand sixteen, I dropped out of MIT, joined Y Combinator, and really started Scale to solve the data pillar of the AI ecosystem and be the organization that was going to solve all the hard problems associated with how to actually produce and create enough data to fuel this ecosystem. And really, this was the start of Scale as the data foundry for AI.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It's incredible foresight because you describe it as like the beginning of the deep learning hype cycle. I don't think most people noticed that the hype cycle was just beginning. And so I just distinctly remember you working through a number of early use cases, building this company in my house at the time, and discovering, I think, far before anybody else noticed, that the autonomous vehicle companies were spending all of their money on data. How did you think about it? Talk a little bit about how the business has evolved since then? Because it's certainly not just that use case.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So you watched the keynote earlier.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: I did. I've never seen a duet before. I've never seen you do it before. You were so synchronized. It seemed like the two of you knew each other. It's incredible. Some... some acquaintance. Okay, you've been doing keynotes a long time. You are, you know, the keynote GOAT. So give us your... we don't have, you know, even a signature outfit yet. We are just amateurs here. So give us this... because you are still young... well, give us your keynote performance review. Like, what would you think?

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I thought it was an A-plus. I thought it was an A-plus. It was really good. You explained perfectly the purpose of the company, what inspires you guys, what keeps you guys up, what makes you work so hard, the ecosystem that you serve, the incredible platform you built, the amazing contribution you make to the world's economy. It's incredible. I thought it was great. There was a whole bunch of technology stuff, feature stuff, money stuff. I didn't understand any of that. But something about a KYC or something. What was that?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: It's a good KYC.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: And thank you so much for taking the time to speak with us.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Of course. Really happy to get to do this.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Awesome. Okay. We have a lot of questions to discuss, so why don't we dive right in? I wanted to start with a sort of high-level question, just to kind of set the tone. So, just in your mind, what would you say are the biggest open questions in AI for people to work on?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: There's still so much. I think we've identified this one incredible thrust of deep learning, and we've seen how far that can go. But I think we are still in the very early innings. The way we build these systems gets so much better every year, and I think that'll keep going for a long time. Data efficiency is still a huge problem. That's still one thing that humans can do very differently than these systems. I think the way that we sort of separate these concepts of pre-training and RL, somehow there's something new to discover there. But I think we could go on for a long time about all the things we still don't know. We have one piece of magic, which is this algorithm that can learn, and we keep finding new ways to do more impressive things with that. It's only been two and a half years since ChatGPT launched, and if you go back and use that original version, it's quite bad. So the rate of progress is still super steep. But the list of problems in front of us and the list of unknowns is still just huge. You know, what it's going to take to build a system that can go autonomously discover new science, which for me is very important to build... still a lot of work to do.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So a lot of people talk about the training data problem, that we are kind of running out of training data. Is that, do you think, an area where there's a need for a lot more work?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: You know, that's another way of saying the data efficiency problem. We are not running out of data if we can learn way, way more from each byte of data. How is it that we are able to learn from just our senses? I would like to know that. Clearly, the way these systems are learning and the way we learn is a little bit, at least a little bit different. Maybe it's very different.

theme: Sam Altman最新 eleven eleven斯坦福访谈

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: The longest shutdown in American history. Democrats' fault. Under your presidency, we are talking about more than a million federal workers who are not getting a paycheck, including our air traffic controllers. You see there's traffic snarls out at the airports now. This weekend, food aid for more than forty-two million Americans is set to expire. What are you doing as president to end the shutdown?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, what we are doing is we keep voting. I mean, the Republicans are voting almost unanimously to end it, and the Democrats keep voting against ending it. You know, they've never had this. This has happened like eighteen times before. The Democrats always voted for an extension, always saying, give us an extension, we'll work it out. They've lost their way. They've become crazed lunatics. And all they have to do, Nora, is say, let's vote.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So, Senate Democrats say they will vote to reopen the government if Republicans agree to extend subsidies for over twenty million Americans who use Obamacare for their health insurance.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Obamacare is terrible. It's bad health care at far too high a price. We should fix that. We can fix it with the Democrats. All they have to do is let the country open and we'll fix it. They have to let the country open and I'll sit down with the Democrats and we'll fix it. But they have to let the country... And you know what they have to do? All they have to do is raise five hands. We don't need all of them.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So you are saying your plan is to tell the Democrats to vote to end the shutdown?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Correct. Very simple.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: And that you will put forward a health care plan?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: No. We will work on fixing the bad health care that we have right now. We have terrible health care that's too expensive for the people. Not for the government, for the people.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Joining us now to discuss this and much more, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. Secretary Bessent, thank you so much for being here.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Always a pleasure.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So let's just start with the SNAP ruling. Two federal judges ruled Friday night the White House must tap into the contingency funds to partially cover SNAP benefits that expired this weekend due to the shutdown. Yesterday, the court clarified that at least a partial payment needs to be made by Wednesday. When can we expect the Trump administration to make these payments?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, President Trump just truth-ed out that he needs to hear from the courts how this is going to be done. And Jake, as you know, the best way for SNAP benefits to get paid is for Democrats, five Democrats, to cross the aisle and reopen the government.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Right, but there is this contingency fund, and as recently as September thirtieth, the Agricultural Department had a memo saying that these funds, the contingency funds, I think it's about five or six billion dollars, could be used to pay these benefits. Now, it would only be for two or three weeks, but that's a lot for people who need the food.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, President Trump just, as I said, President Trump just truth-ed out that he's very anxious to get this done, and it's got to go through the courts. The courts keep jamming up things. Democrats are in the middle of a civil war, and they should just open the government. That is the easiest way to do this.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Is the administration going to appeal the ruling by the judge? Is that what you mean by the courts need to weigh in? Because the courts have weighed in.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: No, but there's a process that has to be followed, so we've got to figure out what the process is. President Trump wants to make sure that people get their food benefits.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So it could be done by Wednesday?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Could be. Okay. Could be, and five Democratic senators could cross the aisle and open the government by Wednesday.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Thank you.

theme: 与财政部长斯科特·贝森特的一对一访谈

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Ladies and gentlemen, we are absolutely thrilled today to be able to welcome Professor Emeritus with the Department of Computer Science at the University of Toronto and Schwartz-Reismann Institute's advisory board member, Geoffrey Hinton. Sir, thank you so much for being with us today.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, thank you so much for inviting me. I am delighted.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: You are known as, and I am sure you will be very demure about this, the godfather of artificial intelligence for your work on neural networks. You co-won the Nobel Prize in Physics in two thousand twenty-four for this work. Is that correct?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: That is correct. It's slightly embarrassing since I don't do physics. So when they called me up and said you won the Nobel Prize in Physics, I didn't believe them to begin with.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: And were the other physicists going, wait a second, that guy's not even in our business?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: I strongly suspect they were, but they didn't say it to me.

theme: AI：哪里可能出错？杰弗里·辛顿访谈 | 乔恩·斯图尔特的每周秀

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: It taught us that this is the fourth industrial revolution and that there is room pretty much for everybody. So, surprising that Lisa Su and AMD team up with OpenAI after you just teamed up, or welcoming to some degree? Because you need to have everybody doing this in order to make it so that you are not the gating factor, because you can't make everybody's chips.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: We could try.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Well, all right. But AMD, Lisa Su... give us a chance.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: You can count on me. You can count on me. Lisa, we are here.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I think she said the MI four hundred fifty is every bit equal to yours.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: And I would say... I want to hear it from Jensen because that's a chip. You are a platform and you are software. You are loaded with software. And so therefore, a piece of hardware may not be equal for total cost of ownership. We do very different things. We build some things that are similar in category, but we are very different companies. NVIDIA started out as a graphics chip company, and over time we became a computing platform company. And a computing platform company is largely software, and you have a lot of developers and ecosystems that create other software that sits on top of your computer. We became a computer platform company. When artificial intelligence came along, we realized that artificial intelligence requires really large computers, and the entire data center is essentially one computer... (Audio becomes unclear and fragmented) ...data center to have data that recognizes... and really webbing... So with that, please sign up. Once again, we've got to change our data here. Great detail here. So we are using that particular interface. If you look up... let's get some data here. You know these data... belords us today... Do I have new data-linked? Yep. I've also been my thirty-seven email list... great data.

theme: 英伟达 CEO 黄仁勋与 Jim Cramer 一对一访谈

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: And I know you also had incredible grandparents and an incredible grandfather. What stays with you today from him, from what he taught you?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, you are right. This is something we have in common. My mother had me when she was very young. She had just turned seventeen years old. She was still in high school in Albuquerque, New Mexico. In fact, they tried to kick her out of high school for being pregnant. And my grandfather said, "No, you can't kick her out. It's a public school. She gets to finish high school." So anyway, there she was, pregnant with me in high school and finishing her whole senior year after I was born. And because she was so young, my grandparents took me every summer so that she could have some other kind of life for a few months. It's not only that she was either. When you are a very, very young mother, you grow up very fast. And so every summer from the age of three or four until I was about sixteen, I went and spent time with my grandparents on their ranch in South Texas. And my grandfather at that point—I only knew him as a rancher. He had done a bunch of things in his life, but at that point he was a rancher. And he did, and this is quite common I think in rural areas, that people do everything themselves. He did his own veterinary work. He repaired everything. He had this D six Caterpillar bulldozer that he had bought for like five thousand dollars because it was completely broken down. And we spent an entire summer fixing it. And to get the transmission out of the thing, we had to build our own crane. And so he had an incredible resourcefulness to him. You know, he believed he could solve any problem. And I sort of watched him—you know, his veterinary work on the cattle, he would make his own needles. He would get a little piece of wire and heat it up with a blowtorch and pound it flat and then sharpen it and drill a little hole through it. But some of the cattle even survived.

theme: 亚马逊创始人杰夫·贝索斯最新现场访谈

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Is some part of their criticism about the situation in Gaza?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Oh, so I am getting—now you can get to—no, well, first of all, I get yelled at about... You know what I get yelled at most about is actually enforcing the border. That's number one. So like, I get yelled atOur southern border.Like, so again, I'll go through all three issues. I get yelled at about—first of all, for decades I got yelled at. So there are legitimate issues to go over, but I just want—for those of you who don't know the history—I've been yelled at for twenty years, and protested primarily for supporting special operations in America. And you just have to imagine that. I am being protested for bringing soldiers home alive and killing our enemies. And these are people who serve our country and have been largely screwed by both parties. Like, both parties have totally screwed them. And so I've been yelled—so that's what I got yelled at. And then I got yelled at about that every single day. Then I got yelled at under Biden, under Obama, Biden, and especially—obviously, if Trump's doing it, you are definitely getting yelled at—for enforcing the border. Now, I got... and here in my life, I got yelled at, and I got yelled at, but this is my favorite thing is that it's always going to have to teach me. The whole call, the subject only has drug... [inaudible] Now, now, I am making a difference. Now, I am making shame all of it, even right now, here in my life.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Thank you so much for being with us. Always great to see you. I am not sure where we should begin because you have such a broad view of these topics. But I feel like we should start with the biggest question, which is, if you look forward at capability growth from here, where does it come from? Does it come from more scale? Does it come from data work?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Multiple vectors of progress. I think there is probably a little bit more juice out of the scalability limit on this piece, so hopefully we'll see some progress there. But it's getting really, really difficult. Society's perception of AI has been very skewed by the PR machinery of a handful of companies with amazing PR capabilities. And because that number of companies drove scale as a narrative, people think of scale first as a vector of progress. But I think agentic workflows, the way we build multimodal models, we have a lot of work to build concrete applications. I mean, there are multiple vectors of progress, as well as wild cards like brand new technologies. Like, can diffusion models, which are used to generate images for the most part, will that also work for generating text? I think that's exciting. So I think there'll be multiple ways for AI to make progress.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: You actually came up with the term "agentic AI." What did you mean then?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: So when I decided to start talking about agentic AI, which wasn't a thing when I started to use the term, my team was slightly annoyed at me. One of my team members that I won't name actually said, "Andrew, the world does not need you to make up another term." But I decided to do it anyway, and for whatever reason, it stuck. The reason I started to talk about agentic AI was because a couple of years ago, I saw people would spend a lot of time debating, is this an agent? Is this not an agent? What is an agent? And I felt there's a lot of good work, and there was a spectrum of degrees of agency—where there are highly autonomous agents that could plan, take multiple steps of reasoning, do a lot of stuff by themselves, and then things that were lower degrees of agency, where we are prompting it, we are affecting its output. And I felt like rather than debating is this an agent or not, let's just talk about the degrees of agency and say it's all agentic, so you can spend our time actually building this. So I started to push the term agentic AI. What I did not expect was that several months later, a bunch of marketers would get a hold of this term and use it as a sticker to stick it on everything in sight. And so I think the term agentic AI really took off.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I feel like the marketing hype has gone insanely fast. But the real business progress has also been rapidly growing, but maybe not as fast as the marketing hype. What do you think are the biggest hype?

theme: 吴恩达 DeepLearning.AI 创始人最新访谈

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Desk Spotlight. Thank you, Jen. I am so happy to be here.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, we are so happy to have you. And here's why I want to start the conversation. If you think and can jog your memory, what's your earliest memory of markets, financial systems, and innovation really mattering to you?

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Oh, goodness. You know, in college, I didn't know what I wanted to do. So I tried everything. I tried engineering and education, geology, astronomy, physics. I just was...

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: You really touched everything.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Oh, my gosh, I did. And one of the reasons I did not take an economics class, probably, truth be told, is my father really wanted me to take an economics class. And so I waited until the last... well, it was the last semester or trimester at UCLA of my sophomore year, took economics, fell in love with it. Realized at UCLA that I wasn't going to be able to take more business classes because they just had a graduate business school, transferred to USC, met Art Laffer—maybe this is TMI, but whatever—met Art Laffer, who is a famous economist, Laffer Curve, who introduced me to Capital Group because he saw how passionate I was about economics. And Capital Group was the largest and most, I would say, probably still is, most prestigious investment firm in Los Angeles. And I walked in, I knew nothing about how the financial world worked. But there, I got the sense of, yes, here's where economics fit. Here's where this exciting market activity is. And, you know, I think I fell in love with the investment world almost as soon as I walked in because I said, wait a minute, I and we are paid for learning, paid to learn. The world is our oyster. This is amazing. We get to figure out how the world's going to work. And I didn't have a doubt in my mind after—I was only twenty years old when I started at Capital—I didn't have a doubt in my mind that I would be in the business for the rest of my life.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: What sparked that passion in economics? I mean, you tried everything, you are rebelling against your dad a little bit. Was it real?

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: We are so close and we were so close, but it's just, you know, kids do that... teenagers do that kind of thing.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: How who we were places imprints on us into who we become. And I wanted to ask you, do you have a childhood experience that you remember that you think has defined who you are today? An experience or a memory that you have that you feel had some coding into who you've become today?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: You know, it's funny you say that because I have a very distinct memory, but for a long time, it was a really sad one. The more I've understood that memory, the more I realized it was kind of where I was at in my life. It was a memory from when I was maybe eight or nine years old. I remember looking outside the window—I come from a big family in the UK, I am one of five, we were really working class, if not poor, we didn't have many luxuries. And life was real. It was very different to LA and sunshine; it was rainy and gray. And I remember sort of looking out the window and I felt like I didn't really belong. I knew I felt different. Like my brothers enjoyed doing football and sports, and my sisters liked doing girls' things like playing with Barbies or braiding hair. And I kind of felt somewhere in the middle. I didn't really feel like I fit in; I was a middle child also. And I remember just looking out and I felt quite alone. And I think that was the beginning of me kind of abandoning myself in a way to fit in, to fit in with what I think people around me thought I should be or who I was told to be. So that memory was, in one way, very motivational now. Now I know what it meant because I can kind of go back to that moment—I've learned that during therapy, being able to connect with that inner child which for so long I ignored. But I think for me, it was a real special moment that I go back to because you can kind of change that. You can change what you came from. And that's a lot about what the book's about—it's about how your roots don't define you. It really is about how we all grow up being told to be something or someone, we have influences around us which kind of lead us into a certain part of our lives. And sometimes we stop and look in the mirror and don't recognize how we got there, let alone how we look. You know, sometimes I've worked with clients and I'll say, you've got short, dark hair and bangs. Why is that? And it's like, well, I've just... what I've always had. I don't know. I am like, have you ever thought about going lighter and longer? Well, no, not really. Why not? Well, just because that's not what... they kind of then stop and go, well, why have I never thought that? Because no one had given them freedom to. And a lot of what my job was about is really opening people up. It really starts on the outside as a visual, but once you start going internal with things and breaking down these boundaries, it's really quite magical what can happen. And that in a nutshell is kind of what I've always done—gone that little bit deeper. And I guess going back to that childhood memory really helps me remember that your roots really don't define you, and you do have a chance to start again. It's never too late. Yeah.

theme: 克里斯·阿普尔顿独家专访：打破沉默，谈论他的离婚以及向他的妻子和孩子们出柜

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So, we are sitting here in New York City in this beautiful new office location. And if you just imagine fifty years ago, we also were in New York City in a slightly different location. You kind of expressed some moments there, but how does that make you feel, just sort of the difference, the locations, where we are today, and everything that you've put into this?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, so it's a lifetime. It's fifty years. And when I started, what I wanted was meaningful work and meaningful relationships. I had no idea how great it could be, how difficult it could be, all of that. So you can imagine fifty years looking back, and this is my extended family. It was raising a family. And so, how does it feel? It feels like the succession. It feels like maybe a father who loves their children and so on, who grow up to be adults. And you can see them flourishing and so on. It feels incredibly rewarding... It's so powerful that comes in with me. I think it feels like it's very, very comfortable, you know. It feels like it's very pleasant. It feels incredibly rewarding. It feels like one of the times.

theme: 瑞·达利欧退休访谈: 回顾桥水公司成立fifty周年

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: You know, with your recent partnership with NVIDIA to ship AI models across Europe, there's been talk about Perplexity being installed on all Samsung phones, pre-installed. And that could lift your valuation towards fourteen billion dollars according to sources like Bloomberg. It's a heavy responsibility being the default search engine for the mainstream population. What do you think are the most important factors at Perplexity to prevent hallucinations or incorrect data from being given to the masses?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Thank you so much. Thank you. Hallucinations is something we really care about. We are building benchmarks internally to keep up to date with that. The only way there is to keep building a better search index, keep capturing better snippets of all the web pages. And then, these models are getting fast enough that you can have them reason multi-step for every query without incurring too much cost. And so that's another way to reduce the hallucinations.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Mr. President, you were just coming off one of the most consequential weeks anybody has ever seen, from striking Iran to going to NATO to announcing two deals, and then the Supreme Court victory. Have you been able to digest what has occurred in the last seven days?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Not really. It was a pretty wild period of time. And I really think in six months, we've taken the country and turned it around. I was told by the King of Saudi Arabia, by the leader of Qatar and the leader of UAE, when we went over there—we worked back a five point one trillion dollar investment into the United States—they said, you know, you are presiding now over the hottest country in the world. And a year ago, we thought your country was dead. It could never come back. It was so incompetently run by a very bad president. And I said, you are right, this country is hot. And that was a couple of months ago. You know, now we are talking about what we've done recently. So the country is really going. The numbers are great. I watch you in the mornings, and you are given those numbers, and they're really good. No inflation. We have a bad Fed chairman. But other than that, we have, you know, great. It doesn't even matter. The numbers are so good, it doesn't matter that he keeps the rates artificially high. But it's been an amazing—it's been an amazing creative time.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: When you went to the Middle East and you strengthened those relationships with all of those countries you just mentioned, did you have an idea that you would have to do something with regard to Iran? Because you teased it a little. You said, look, they've got to come to the table and talk. Something's going to happen.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, it was getting a little bit hot. And we knew they had a lot of sites, probably three plus the one. But they had three main sites, and we knew they were going to have to either give them up. And I thought we could do it during negotiation, and we just about had it done. And then they said, we want enrichment. Enrichment doesn't mean like air conditioning, and it doesn't mean to jack up your car. Enrichment is a bad word. And I said, you've got so much oil. What do you need that for? And they said, well, we need it. We need it. And I wouldn't let that happen.

theme: 特朗普最新专访谈论美国对伊朗的袭击：“如黄油般顺滑”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Done in Argentina. What is your take on that bill, but more importantly, what it represents, which is the global fight against national debt? I would love to get your take on it, on what the world can learn from what you've achieved.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Let's see. There's a phrase from one of my ministers, who is the minister of deregulation. And he says that the Argentine case basically demonstrated three things that no one could imagine and that no one believed were possible. Basically, the first point is the size of the fiscal adjustment and the timing. Dr. Sturzenegger usually says that many presidents ask him, well, we have a fiscal imbalance, we would need to replicate what Milei is doing in Argentina. And then he says, well, how much do you have to adjust? Then they say, well, four percent of the deficit. And he says, well, how much would you be willing to adjust? One point a year. And Federico Sturzenegger answers him, well, why not five in a month? I mean, we made a fiscal adjustment of five points of GDP in a month. In strict rigor, the adjustment was of seven points of GDP. But as we knew that we were starting from a situation of little confidence, of little credibility, because nobody believed that this kind of thing could be done. We were coming from a situation where Argentina had had a fiscal deficit in the last one hundred and twenty-three previous years. The only times it had had a fiscal surplus was because it was not paying its debt. So, in that context, it was very difficult to believe that something of these characteristics could be done. And we knew that this could have a negative impact on activity, on employment, and the number of poor. And that too, we had a very aggressive program on social containment.

theme: 哈维尔·米莱总统访谈：阿根廷自救、特朗普、以色列及更多..

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Dr. Gao, on Tuesday, China accused U.S. President Trump of pouring oil on the conflict there between Iran and Israel. Why and how does Beijing see the United States making things worse with regards to this situation?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, I think evidence points to a joint operation by the United States and Israel against the country of Iran. They probably have a division of work. The United States was not very much involved on the surface of the situation. But no one should be surprised if the United States highly coordinates its information gathering, etc., with Israel. And the fact that Israel has kept saying that the United States is involved from the very beginning of the war, etc., illustrates the level of cooperation and collaboration between the United States and Israel against Iran. And the United States still keeps saying that they are not involved, but then they need to come up with the proof that they are not involved. And not being involved is not sufficient. And the United States really needs to pull all its levers to force Israel to stop this war against Iran and also to stop Israel from misusing the United States, creating all the misinformation about the United States and Israel working together to, for example, destroy Iran or to create a regime change in Iran, etc. So I think it is time for the United States. My personal concern is that if the United States really gets bogged down in this war against Iran, no one knows what will happen to the United States in the coming years or coming decades.

theme: 高志凯教授访谈: 以色列拥核还指责伊朗太虚伪：“中国不希望看到对伊朗的侵略战争”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Let's start way back. I mean, when you were... this is a room full of eighteen to twenty-five year olds. It skews younger because the founder set is younger and younger. Can you put yourself back into their shoes when you were eighteen, nineteen, learning to code, even coming up with a first idea for Zip2? What was that like for you?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Yeah, back in ninety-five, I was faced with a choice of either do grad studies PhD at Stanford in material science, actually working on ultra-capacitors for potential use in electric vehicles, essentially trying to solve the range problem for electric vehicles, or try to do something in this thing that most people have never heard of called the internet. I talked to my professor who was Bill Nix in the material science department and said, can I defer for the quarter because this will probably fail and then I'll need to come back to college? And he said, this is probably the last conversation we'll have. And he was right. So I thought things would most likely fail, not that they would most likely succeed. Then in ninety-five, I wrote basically, I think the first or close to the first maps, directions, internet, white pages and yellow pages on the internet. I just wrote that personally. I didn't even use a web server. I just read the port directly. Because I couldn't afford a T one. The original office was on Sherman Avenue in Palo Alto. There was like an ISP on the floor below. So I drilled a hole through the floor and just ran a LAN cable directly to the ISP. And my brother joined me and another co-founder, Greg Curry, who passed away. At the time, we couldn't even afford a place to stay. So the office was five hundred bucks a month. We just slept in the office and then showered at the YMCA on Page Mill and El Camino. And yeah, we ended up doing a little bit of a useful company at the beginning... (Audio becomes unclear and fragmented with unintelligible speech)

theme: 马斯克最新访谈：在智能爆炸前夜，我们是数字超级智能的“生物引导程序”

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Let's start by talking about the future of AI. And I want to talk about the medium term because the short term is not as interesting to me. The long term, who knows? But like five, ten years out is what I am most interested in talking about. And I kind of want to try to pull out from you your best guess on a bunch of specific things. One of the places I wanted to start was in software. It seems like the most effective use cases so far, which I am curious if you agree with, but seem to be coding and then chat. Yeah, chat and code. I am curious what's next? Like, what's the next set of things right after that that will come?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, I think there will be incredible other products, like there will be crazy new social experiences. There will be like Google Docs style AI workflows that are just way more productive. You'll start to see like you'll have these virtual employees. But the thing that I think will be the most impactful on that five to ten year timeframe is AI will actually discover new science. And this is a crazy claim to make, but I think it is true. And if it is correct, then over time, I think that will dwarf everything else.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Why do you think it'll discover new science?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, I think we've cracked reasoning in the models. We have a long way to go, but I think we know what to do. And you know, O three is already pretty smart. You hear people say like, wow, this is like a good PhD.

theme: OpenAI萨姆·奥尔特曼：人工智能的未来

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Why were you being held against your will by the French government? In the first four days, did they make it clear to you?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: So I understood that they were worried about the alleged lack of response from Telegram towards the judicial requests coming from France, which turned out to be not true because we've never received a single legally binding legal request from France. So I was even more confused. And I asked the French policeman, why haven't you been following the European law and serving your legal requests in the way prescribed by this law? It's defined by the Digital Services Act and it's there. You can Google the process. You can read the privacy policy of Telegram. It's easily accessible on the website. So why didn't you do it? And they didn't answer. But thank God they started to do it. And then they started to receive responses, helping them to identify criminal suspects, meaning we would disclose IP address and phone number of people who were suspects in criminal investigations when we received court orders signed by a judge. Right. And this was something that we had in place since a year ago before that.

theme: 卡尔斯访谈：电报创始人杜罗夫自他在法国被逮捕软禁以来首次发声

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Bill, as Trump calls it, has passed the House. The Senate's now wrestling with it. It seems like it would add maybe two point four trillion dollars to the deficit by the last estimates. Is that a really bad thing? Should we worry about that bill adding too much to the deficit? Now, you say you got to bring it down by more than half the amount of the deficit. Can that be done with spending cuts alone, or do you think they should let some of the tax cuts expire?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: I think that if there's one thing I can give people, and I am compelled to give people now, it's an understanding of the mechanics and the supply and demand so that people will understand how to answer that question, which is why, as a global investor for fifty years who's been through many of these, I wanted to pass along my book, my description of the mechanics. In answering your question, I want to say why it's a compelling moment, and that if they do not bring it down to three percent of GDP, from where it'll be about seven percent of GDP, they have to bring it down by four percent of GDP, that there is very high likelihood of real problems. It has to be done by three things. It has to be spread out among these three things, because any one of those three things would be too painful. Those three things are tax revenue, spending cuts, and interest rates. Although Congress and the President and the process does not deal directly with the third of those, right now, a trillion dollars, half of our deficit is interest payments.

theme: 瑞·达利欧最新访谈：若无债务解决方案，美国经济将"撞礁"

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: You have made extraordinary contributions to science and policy over the past two decades. I'll start with the biggest question: why start a company now?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Because in my heart, I want to build. I see this as such a critical, fun, and exciting moment to build some extraordinary technology that everybody can use. And I believe so much in spatial intelligence and the kind of three D world models that can empower so many people as well as so many use cases. And I think that's just going to be really exciting. And I can do that with an extraordinarily brilliant group of young technologists.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: I want to come back to the people you are working with, because I know some of your co-founders and was trying to convince them desperately to start a company a while back. And then they were like, "Oh no, we have a bigger mission now with Fei-Fei." What is spatial intelligence? Can you define it for a broader audience?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Spatial intelligence to me is the ability to understand, reason, interact, and generate three D worlds. So, if you don't have the capacity, we can only interact in limited ways, and it can cause anxiety, but we can navigate many scenarios over the years. But for us, it's just a matter of power and there's choice. So, some of us don't have the chance to use it to become a bigger player. It's a large problem now with a pretty thin case that we have to work with. Also, the profound nature of some companies is due to the importance among everyone coming to us for physical robotics, and it's just kind of ironic that we have to view it that way. But if you want more colleagues in the field.

theme: 李飞飞访谈 |斯坦福大学HAI及世界实验室创始人

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: As you know very well, Mr. President, the Trump administration has taken a number of actions against Harvard University. They've cut off grants in a number of different ways. And most recently, they've ordered all international students to leave the university for some other university or for their home countries. In your lawsuit, the most recent of your lawsuits about this, you argue that this isn't really about international students, that it's an act of retaliation. What is really going on in your view?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: In my view, the federal government is saying that we need to address anti-Semitism in particular, but it has raised other issues, including issues about speech. And it includes claims that we lack viewpoint diversity. There are also claims about failure to comply with laws concerning discrimination more generally. For some of this, we have been very clear that we think we do have issues. And I would particularly emphasize the speech issues. We think it's a real problem if, particularly at research universities, students don't feel free to speak their minds. When faculty feel that they have to think twice before they talk about the subjects that they're teaching, that's a real problem that we need to address. And it's particularly concerning when people who have views that they think are unpopular. And the administration and others have said conservatives are too few on campus and their views are not welcome. And as far as that's true, that's a problem we really need to address. Is it true? I think that we have heard from some people that they do feel that way. So we certainly need to address that. And that means changing views in the community, making diverse viewpoints more welcome. It includes skills in speaking as well as skills in listening.

theme: 哈佛校长访谈：有时他们不喜欢我们所代表的东西

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So I know you guys have screened other Black Mirror episodes at the great BFI before, but this one feels particularly appropriate. Did you always envision it on the big screen?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: I mean, yes. No. I mean, it's kind of a... It is a love letter to classic films, of which I've probably not seen enough. So this felt like the perfect sort of venue to show it. I hope people think we did the vintage movie industry proud. And so it just felt like a... It feels like a natural hope. Yeah.

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: And two films in particular, right? There's the characters, when they're describing the original Hotel Reverie, they reference Casablanca, the nineteen forty-two film with Ingrid Bergman and Humphrey Bogart, and Brief Encounter, the nineteen forty-five David Lean film. What was it that you wanted to take from those films?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well, especially... So I should say, so originally, it's weird. So how the episodes come about is sometimes quite odd. So initially, I was thinking... I had two ideas in my head. One was I knew for quite a long time that I wanted to do a horror story about somebody using technology to restore an old black and white silent horror film and they discover they can talk to people in the film. This was one idea I had, but I wasn't quite sure what the story was beyond one sort of creepy moment. And then I had another idea, which was, well, what if there was a high-tech way of replacing an actor in a big action movie, like a sort of Bond movie, and the wrong sort of character is thrown into that. And so... Do you think that was a bit strange? And you certainly have tried that long because something like a weird movie has been quite a hype for a while. And then I was actually putting this together about an idea of the film's plot. To aside, all this might have been a little bit vague and I can change that. And... So here I think it was another avenue to help with that. If that was a part of my idea that had to go, and maybe I can change that way, and the next direction would always be and probably have been the overall, and the next endeavor to work.

theme: 艾玛·科林、查理·布鲁克以及《黑镜：酒店幻梦》的制作人

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: So you've come here at a time when the IMF has just announced this twenty billion dollar agreement with Argentina. But we haven't seen a Treasury secretary come to Argentina since Trump's first administration. And that was for a G twenty. So why the emphasis from the Trump administration on Argentina. When it comes to the trade negotiations. I am sure you spoke with President Milei about the ten percent rate that Argentina was hit with. Do you think at some point that can come down to zero. Can any country go to zero.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Well and Marie a couple of reasons. The reason for being here today is today's a fulcrum day. So the Milei administration has done three adjustments and this marks the beginning of the third one. So they did a large fiscal adjustment or monetary adjustment. And they're doing a lot. They announced on Friday a large currency adjustment. IMF has allocated twenty billion to them. The World Bank is allocated twelve billion. And I wanted to come here today to show support for President Milei and his commitment. The move I think is historic in terms of bringing Argentina back from the precipice. Look I think we are going to start the negotiations. And just like with everyone else I am telling them bring your A-game. We'll see what you've got. And we'll go from there. Well again we'll see. It is. I don't know what's going to happen with the negotiations because we've got a whole box of things we've got to overcome tariff non tariff trade barriers currency manipulation and subsidization of labor and facilities. So there's a big menu there.

theme: 彭博：美财长访谈：贸易谈判、鲍威尔的未来、阿根廷、美元

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: From our professional experience, over sixty percent of our time and energy is spent providing legal services to private entrepreneurs in China. This includes defending them when they’re arrested, as well as helping them seek justice if they’ve been wronged. It’s simple really—Chinese entrepreneurs usually have the means to pay. Of course, we also take on cases for the less fortunate, and occasionally political cases.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: So basically, you’re using the income from corporate clients and entrepreneurs to cover the cases that don’t make money, right?

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Exactly. In our own words, we call it “taking from the rich to help the poor.” We use the money from entrepreneurs to assist the underprivileged. Sometimes, I even justify it for myself: these private entrepreneurs, during their prime, don’t fully appreciate the importance of the legal system in China, so in a way, they’re paying for their own understanding—and at the same time, contributing to society and the rule of law. They pay legal fees, and lawyers can then help the less fortunate. It feels like a balanced approach.

theme: 民营企业家接连自杀背后

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: It really feels like the moment has arrived. But there’s still a lot of debate — has the metaverse already come? Are we actually living in it right now?

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Definitely not yet. Spielberg’s Ready Player One gives us a vivid picture of what a true metaverse might look like — a world that’s beautifully designed and endlessly fascinating. But that kind of immersive experience is still far away. Maybe five years if we’re lucky, or ten to fifteen years if development takes longer. It’s going to take time. When people say “the metaverse has arrived,” they mostly mean that last year was seen as the “first year of the metaverse.” It was the year people started widely accepting this new idea — the Metaverse — and began exploring what it could be. And among those people, including myself, there’s a strong belief that one day, it really will come to life.

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Interesting. At the end of last year, when you launched your “Xirang” project, you said there were too many bubbles around the metaverse concept, and that you believed the bubble would burst this year. Just now, you said last year was the start of the metaverse era — and this year, the bubble bursts? How should we understand that?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: You mentioned students earlier — just to add, you once worked as an associate professor at the Cheung Kong School of Journalism and Communication at Shantou University, right?

content.speaker: Speaker2
content.text: Sorry for interrupting. I was saying — I often meet young people who were, in a way, shaped by that early wave of public intellectual culture on Weibo, around ten years ago. What are they like now?

content.speaker: Speaker1
content.text: Back in that time — around two thousand and twelve — I actually started quite early. I was part of a small journalist group focused on gender equality, called New Media Women. It later became an NGO. We were among the first to take part in grassroots feminist activism in China.
